era_context: Continuation of the Neo-Assyrian expansion under Ashurnasirpal II. The Levantine states remain under increasing Assyrian pressure. Egypt is fragmented. Greece continues in the Geometric period. The Shang Dynasty has ended in China (traditionally dated to 1046 BCE), and the Zhou Dynasty is well-established, though the Western Zhou period is ending and the Eastern Zhou is beginning (traditionally 771 BCE). This is a period of relative stability in the Near East before the major upheavals of the late 9th century.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese records exist but specific year 873 BCE is not well-integrated into Western scholarship)
South Asia (Vedic period; no written records for specific events)

events.title: Continuation of Ashurnasirpal II's Levantine campaigns and consolidation
events.description: Ashurnasirpal II continues his military campaigns in the Levant, further consolidating Assyrian control and extracting tribute from vassal states. The campaigns are documented in royal inscriptions that record the subjugation of various city-states and the imposition of Assyrian administrative control. These campaigns establish the infrastructure of Assyrian imperial rule that will persist for the next century.
events.certainty_note: Ashurnasirpal II's reign and general campaign activities are well-documented, but the specific events of individual years within his reign are sometimes difficult to date precisely. 873 BCE falls within his documented campaign period.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the general narrative of uninterrupted Assyrian expansion can obscure periods of resistance and local resilience by Levantine states, which are underrepresented in the surviving sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 873 BCE is even sparser than for 874 BCE. We are almost entirely dependent on Assyrian royal inscriptions, which are propagandistic and do not provide a balanced view of the period. Local perspectives from the Levantine states are largely absent. The record is heavily skewed toward military and political events involving elites; economic, social, and cultural developments are largely invisible unless they appear in archaeological evidence.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of the same campaign cycle documented in 874 BCE
